summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Microsoft's greatest threat is not external competitors but its own internal bureaucracy and slow development cycles. Writing at the end of 2005, he notes that five years have passed since Windows XP—the longest gap between Windows releases ever. He contrasts Microsoft's glacial pace with nimble web companies like Salesforce and Amazon, where engineers can ship fixes to millions of users overnight. While acknowledging Microsoft's efforts at transparency through blogging, Atwood contends these cultural changes won't matter unless the company fundamentally accelerates its software development cycle.
key_points: Microsoft succeeds not through monopoly power but by releasing decent, usable products—yet its massive size has become its own worst enemy
The five-year gap since Windows XP represents Microsoft's longest-ever release cycle, symptomatic of organizational sclerosis
Web-based competitors like Salesforce can iterate constantly while Microsoft takes years between traditional software updates
Microsoft's adoption of transparency and blogging is positive but insufficient without faster shipping cycles
The company rewards process excellence and risk-aversion over actually shipping features, creating a culture where 'No can ship!' dominates
money_quotes: Microsoft is far from perfect, but the alternatives were always so much worse.
Microsoft, the company everyone loves to hate, has adopted transparency and blogging aggressively.

If you openly reward and promote people for killing work by bemoaning the risk and the testing cost and localization impact of each feature... everyone is going to grab pitchforks and jump on that 'No can do! No can ship!' bandwagon.

By the next morning millions of users had benefited from his work. Not a single customer had to download a bag of bits, answer any silly questions, prove that they are not software thieves, reboot their computers, etc.
I think Microsoft is doing the right things – but they need to do them a lot faster.
tone: critical but constructive, with sardonic humor
key_insight: Large software companies don't die from external competition—they die from internal bureaucracy that makes them too slow to respond to a world that moves faster every year.
